title: Lira Pressure, Regional Nerves, and a New Party on the Scene
overview: The lira remains the story that touches daily life most directly today, with inflation expectations and Middle East tensions keeping the currency under pressure and rate cuts off the table. Regionally, rhetoric around the Strait of Hormuz is sharpening, though Türkiye itself remains at a steady travel-advisory level. Domestically, a new opposition party has entered the field ahead of what promises to be a busier political season.

sections.heading: Money: Lira Under Pressure, No Rate Relief in Sight
sections.body: The dollar has strengthened further against the lira (USD/TRY at 47.2), and Commerzbank and other analysts point to rising inflation expectations as the driver. Bloomberg reports Turkey's central bank will likely hold rates steady, as the Middle East conflict complicates the disinflation path. Fitch has said sustained foreign-reserve buildup remains key to any ratings upgrade, while Citigroup has cut its Turkish bond position by half, citing the oil price surge. For residents, this means continued planning around currency volatility; those with dollar or euro savings, or income from abroad, will notice the gap widening. Separately, BKM data show Turkish cardholders spent a record 93.1 billion lira abroad in May — a sign that spending power at home is pushing people to convert and travel.

sections.heading: Hormuz Tensions: Rhetoric Rises, Türkiye Stays Calm
sections.body: President Trump has said the US will strike an Iranian bridge or power plant for every attack on shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, a warning covered widely by AP, Axios, and the BBC. Anadolu Ajansı reports Iran's countering warning that any infrastructure strike would trigger a 'powerful' response. State-aligned Turkish coverage has also highlighted Iran's statement that Turkey helped prevent a Kurdish-group offensive from northern Iraq during the recent conflict — a claim opposition-leaning Turkish Minute reports with more caution about Iranian framing. None of this changes Türkiye's own travel-advisory level (still L2), but it underscores why regional shipping and insurance costs remain elevated, and why longer land/air routes around Hormuz are being discussed as alternatives.

sections.heading: Politics: Ozel Launches a New Party
sections.body: DW reports that Özgür Özel has launched a new party aimed at challenging President Erdoğan, adding a fresh player to Turkish politics. It's early days — no policy detail yet reported — but worth watching for anyone tracking the country's political direction over the coming term.

sections.heading: Migration Notes
sections.body: Germany's deportation of a Uyghur man to China, reported by his mother, has drawn attention internationally, alongside a separate case of a Turkish man deported from the US despite prior torture protections. Both stories are reminders that deportation and asylum protections vary sharply by country, relevant for anyone navigating cross-border residency questions. Meanwhile, a funding call for refugee and asylum-seeker protection services inside Turkey signals continued NGO activity in this space.

closing: Keep an eye on the exchange rate today, and safe travels wherever you're headed this week.
